| 296 | **‚òÖ SEEDED-RNG KEYSTONE ‚Äî `rng.ts` runs on `Math.random()`, not a seeded generator, despite CLAUDE.md mandating "seeded RNG; keep engine code deterministic"; the d100/rollVs mechanic EXISTS but is non-reproducible ‚Äî the HARD PREREQUISITE for a deterministic/testable CPU and for the whole gap log's "resolve every decision to a die roll" objective ‚Äî NEW (b50, `c8d89e49` CPU-rules)** | **Code-verified: `src/rng.ts` uses `Math.random()` directly** (its own header: "deterministic seeding can be plugged in later"). It DOES expose `d100()` and `rollVs(target): {roll, success, margin}` ‚Äî the EXACT d100-vs-threshold mechanic the CPU-rules thread requires ‚Äî but `rand()`/`d()`/`pick`/`shuffle`/`pickWeighted`/`uid` are all `Math.random`-backed, so NO engine roll is reproducible. The contradiction is doc-wide: game-context.md's own preamble (and CLAUDE.md) ASSERT seeded RNG as the design assumption, but it is unbuilt. (Several other rows independently flag this `Math.random` violation in their own mutators ‚Äî `calcStateVote` die #292/#287, the generator #290 ‚Äî this is the shared root.) | **The CPU-rules thread's OBJECTIVE 1 (`c8d89e49` POST 1-2) is "make the CPU rules require absolutely no human interpretation ‚Äî '20% it does this, 40% that' ‚Äî then you just roll a die and do it," i.e. every CPU rule resolves to a SINGLE d100 against cumulative percentage bands.** For that objective (and the deterministic CPU gov-action spec #20, and reproducible/testable playtests in the browser port) the d100 rolls MUST run on a seeded generator. `rollVs(target)` is already the correct shape ‚Äî it just needs a seeded backend. This is a PORT-WIDE keystone: it underwrites every probabilistic gap (draft, ideology-shift, conversion, kingmaker, elections, war, gov-actions) being verifiable | Back `d100()`/`rollVs()`/`rand()`/`pick`/`shuffle`/`pickWeighted` with a SEEDED generator (the deterministic RNG CLAUDE.md already mandates); thread a per-game seed through the snapshot so every CPU d100 + every engine roll is reproducible. This is a HARD PREREQUISITE for #20 (deterministic CPU gov-actions) and for OBJECTIVE 1 being verifiable, and it removes the `Math.random` determinism bugs other rows flag (#292/#287/#290). Lowest-level keystone ‚Äî schedule before the deterministic-CPU work. Cross-ref #20, #292, #290, and the doc preamble's seeded-RNG assertion | ingested (**`c8d89e49` "Need Your Input: Fixing CPU Rules" POST 1, 2 (OBJECTIVE 1 = "roll a die and do it"); codebase-verified `src/rng.ts` uses `Math.random()` (header "deterministic seeding can be plugged in later"), exposes `d100()`/`rollVs()` (correct mechanic) but all helpers `Math.random`-backed. Contradicts CLAUDE.md + this doc's preamble (lines 257, 340). OPEN: per-game seed plumbing through the snapshot**; **‚òÖ (b51) `8a0a14a1` (structural-flexibility wish-list) re-confirms this keystone from the Convention/CPU-vote angle: a "radically different Constitutional Convention" playtest (the thread's #1 wish, POST 2) is NOT reproducible/tunable today because every Convention + CPU vote resolves via `chance()`/`pick()` ‚Üí `Math.random()` (`rng.ts:4-6, 45-47`) ‚Äî `voteCC` (`continentalCongress.ts:211-215`) and `autoFillCPUVotes`/`preferredOption` (`constitutionalConvention.ts:81-123`) are all `Math.random`-backed coin-flips. So the seeded-RNG keystone underwrites not just deterministic CPU vote weighting (#74) but the whole "run a counterfactual Convention and tune it" use case players explicitly want. Same root as `c8d89e49`. `8a0a14a1` POST 2**; **‚òÖ (b52) ANOTHER `Math.random()` determinism site found: `applyCasualties` picks battle victims with `Math.random()` (`revolutionaryWar.ts:89,97`), violating the seeded-RNG rule (the rest of the file uses `d`/`d100`/`chance`/`pick` from `rng.ts`). Any retune of the casualty system (#85) must also fix the RNG source. `c4eadba5` SHIPPED-vs-DESIGN ¬ß (Determinism caution)**; **‚òÖ (b55) the community's hand-built automation scripts are a de-facto WITNESS that the engine is roll-driven THROUGHOUT ‚Äî `working-towards-automation-scripts` (1bb2ac6b, GA-built tooling, POST 1-47): every script the forum wrote (committee gains, leader rolls, career gains, battle sim, deaths/retirements, pol generator, drafts) **performs dice rolls** the in-app port must reproduce, confirming a reproducible seeded RNG is required across the whole subsystem set; `src/rng.ts:1-5` is still `Math.random`-backed (re-verified b55). The pol generator was generated from **rules 3.0.25** (POST 44) ‚Äî a late-2025/early-2026 rules-doc version pin. Corroborates this keystone from the tooling side. `working-towards-automation-scripts` POST 1, 22, 30, 44, 46**; **‚òÖ (b58) the designer's election changelog makes this keystone LOAD-BEARING for a CURRENTLY-LANDING change: `97aa3f6f` ("State of the Rules") POST 5/6 introduces explicit **50%-chance-+1** (per trait, #189) and **20%√óability-chance-+1** (Gov/Sen, #292) election ROLLS ‚Äî these must run on seeded `chance(...)`, and they likely SUPERSEDE the `(Math.random()-0.5)*8` jitter in `calcStateVote` (`phaseRunners.ts:3711`), one more concrete `Math.random` site this keystone must replace. Adopting the changelog's new probabilistic election model WITHOUT the seeded backend would make every election non-reproducible. `97aa3f6f` POST 5, 6**) |
| 291 | **‚òÖ IDEOLOGY-MODEL gap ‚Äî no axis/flag for non-ideological "pure" (ambition / personal-gain-driven) populism; such figures get pulled to the CENTER and mis-rated as moderate ‚Äî DESIGNER-DEFERRED (a known accuracy ceiling, not a bug) ‚Äî NEW (b48, `036f0dce`)** | **The shipped 7-point `Ideology` scale has no such axis.** Code-verified: `Ideology = 'LW Populist' \| 'Progressive' \| 'Liberal' \| 'Moderate' \| 'Conservative' \| 'Traditionalist' \| 'RW Populist'` (`types.ts:5-12`, exact match to the model the thread critiques); there is NO non-ideological / personal-gain populism value and no such `Trait` (`types.ts:62-117`). Two fix options on record: **(cheap/preferred)** keep the 7-point scale, force a left/right-populist call, and add a dedicated "pure / non-ideological populist" TRAIT to flag the ambition-driven distinction (POST 14); **(expensive)** add a new axis to the ideology model (POST 12-13). BOTH **deferred behind a "many more Gabbards" trigger** ‚Äî "I'll change the model if we get many more Gabbards. She's sort of like a 1 in 1,000 case" (OrangeP47 POST 13); "not enough pols that fit ‚Ä¶ to make it worthwhile at this point" (POST 14). Record as a KNOWN accuracy ceiling, DEFERRED. Roadmap relevance is contingent ‚Äî revisit only if extreme-but-non-ideological figures become common (later/populist-heavy eras). Pairs with the snapshot-classification convention (active-figure ratings are deliberately provisional, POST 7) | NO immediate build action ‚Äî recorded as a deferred design limitation. IF the modern/populist roster grows enough to trip the "many more Gabbards" trigger: add either a non-ideological-populist TRAIT (cheap, a left/right-pop call + a flag) or a new ideology AXIS (expensive). OPEN (human call): is the deferral still in force? has the Trump-era + later roster grown enough to justify it? A bundle bill = a CONTAINER of N independently-authored sub-effects (each a normal bill-sized effect) voted as one; designer-fixed components = Medicaid cuts + tax changes (POST 20), community-proposed = ICE funding, **state-targeted swing-vote exemptions** (Alaska/Hawaii carve-outs, POST 21), 10-yr AI-regulation preemption, bar non-US DOJ settlements, EV/hybrid registration grants (POST 22). DISTINCT from #221 (the content-primitive registry ‚Äî a bundle is a NEW bill TYPE on top) and from the composite `EraEventResponseEffect` (which carries multiple sub-effects inside ONE response but is NOT a set of separately-VOTABLE components). Committee side-effects + **faction points awarded in the Executive Phase as if the bill were signed, REGARDLESS of guilt**. This is the DESIGN HOME for scandal‚Üíremoval and the natural PRODUCER of the dead `forceRetire` executor (QW49). ‚òÖ PAPER-RULESET bug (NOT an in-app DH): bands 15 and 20 overlap verbatim ("15-20"/"21-25") ‚Üí a build must pick half-open bands. | 331 | **‚òÖ NOMINATION-MECHANISM ERA-SELECTOR + the CONGRESSIONAL NOMINATING CAUCUS ("King Caucus") band ‚Äî an era branch chooses pre-12A-spoiler / caucus / convention / primary; ~1804-1830s nominations run by each party's Congress members ‚Äî NEW (b58, `fb8070f3`)** | **0% built ‚Äî there is ONE nomination path with no era branch.** Nomination phases (2.9.1 Primaries ‚Üí 2.9.2 Conventions) fire only in presidential years via `isPresidentialYear` = `year % 4 === 0` (`phases.ts:53-55,69-70`) with **no era branch** selecting caucus-vs-convention-vs-primary. `runPhase_2_9_1_Primaries` is a single-PV-winner sort (`phaseRunners.ts:3725-3750`); 2.9.2 is a one-line log stub (`engine.ts:69`). **No Congressional Nominating Caucus path anywhere** (grep `caucus\|nominatingCaucus` in `src/` = 0; the term appears only as a flavor blurb, `FactionLeaderPage.tsx:12`) | **Matt's rulebook (`fb8070f3` POST 7) defines a 4-row era table for WHICH nominating mechanism applies: pre-12A (‚â§1804) = the old two-votes-per-elector / spoiler rules (owned on #72); **post-12A ‚Üí ~1830s = a Congressional Nominating Caucus ("King Caucus")** ‚Äî Pres+VP nominated by each party's senators+congressmen, "basically just use the same logic as congress positions"; **~1830s ‚Üí pre-state-primaries = National Convention** ("normalization of conventions in the 1830s via event probably" ‚Äî the system owned on #185); primary era = primaries‚Üíconvention (the shipped stubbed path). The 12th Amendment is the hinge that makes electors vote separately for Pres+VP, which is WHY a convention forms a deliberate P+VP ticket. The era-selector (a single gate choosing the mechanism by era/event) and the King Caucus mechanism are NOT previously captured in the KB as mechanics (the caucus term appears only as historical background). DISTINCT from #185 (the convention system itself), #71/#72 (the CPU convention decision spec + eligibility/VP), #183 (endorsement‚Üímomentum): this row is the ERA-BRANCH that selects which of those runs + the earliest (caucus) mechanism the branch can pick. The shipped `isPresidentialYear`/`shouldRunPhase` has no era branch. Pairs with #185 (convention) / #72 (pre-12A spoiler + VP eligibility) / #92 (era axis the branch keys on) | ingested (**`fb8070f3` (Post-12th Amendment: Pre-primary conventions, matthewyoung123) POST 7 ‚Äî the 4-row era‚Üímechanism table + the Congressional Nominating Caucus ("King Caucus") spec; codebase-verified the nomination phases have NO era branch (`phases.ts:53-55,69-70`, `isPresidentialYear`), no caucus path (grep=0, only a flavor blurb `FactionLeaderPage.tsx:12`), 2.9.1 single-winner sort + 2.9.2 log stub. Pairs with #185/#72/#92**; **‚òÖ (b60) `0adcd14c` (Presidential Election Question, Apr 2024) gives the primary-era boundary a CONCRETE downstream consumer + its canonical DEFINITION: the general-election "Give a Speech" speaker-eligibility rule FLIPS on the **primary era = "‚â•1 state has a primary"** (POST 1, designer-confirmed POST 2) ‚Äî the exact boundary this era-selector must expose. Build re-verified has NO such detection (`hasPrimary`/"‚â•1 state has a primary" = 0 in `src/`; the shipped `presPrimary` phase is unconditional, NOT the historical primary-era boundary). Confirms the era-selector must gate not just caucus/convention/primary NOMINATION but also downstream campaign-action eligibility (speech speaker = nominee vs VP/faction-leader, owned on #185). `0adcd14c` POST 1, 2**; **‚òÖ (b64) `8477b37b` ("Primary question") fleshes out THIS selector's PRIMARY branch + pins the primary-era ACTIVATION trigger: the primary era begins **once a governor ACTIVATES primaries** ‚Äî "activating primaries" is a GOVERNOR ACTION (POST 2), the first datum tying the governor-action system (#20) to the nomination machine and the concrete realization of this row's "primary era = ‚â•1 state has a primary" boundary (#331/`0adcd14c`). So the era-selector's primary branch is not a fixed calendar ‚Äî it is governor-triggered and only live post-activation. | 332 | **‚òÖ MID-GAME (CRISIS) CONSTITUTIONAL CONVENTION subsystem ‚Äî a Domestic-Stability-lowest-triggered path to REWRITE the government mid-scenario: a per-state "call a convention" gov-action gated on Governor 5-Gov, a 2/3-of-states threshold RACED against Dom-Stab recovery, + a scenario-boot-AT-a-ConCon variant ‚Äî NEW (b59, `5c3a92a9`)** | **0% built ‚Äî only the FOUNDING 1787/1788 convention ships.** `makeConvention(year)` (`constitutionalConvention.ts:6-77`, the 7-article convention) has ONE caller: the hardcoded 1772-scenario era-event case `constitutional_convention_kickoff` (`phaseRunners.ts:3182-3186`, reached via the Annapolis graph `eraEvents1772.ts:389-421`). **NO Dom-Stab trigger** ‚Äî `meters.domestic` (`types.ts:1403`) is read/written ONLY by bill effects + cabinet-drift ticks (`phaseRunners.ts:3218-3219,3287,3302,3350,2910-2947`); nothing reads a low value to unlock/gate/trigger a convention. **NO "call a convention" gov/state action** ‚Äî the 2.5.2 Governors phase is the flat 30%-skill-nudge stub (`phaseRunners.ts:3382-3392`; grep `callConvention\|proposeConvention\|reformGovernment` = 0). **NO 5-Gov gate, NO 2/3-convention threshold** (grep `governing >= 5`/`5 Gov` = 0; the `2/3` constants in code are unrelated ‚Äî Articles-of-Confederation bill passage `continentalCongress.ts:198` + the `amendmentProcess` article option `'two_thirds'` `constitutionalConvention.ts:46`). The `startConvention` postEffect is a DEAD type-union member (`types.ts:1478`; the dispatcher `phaseRunners.ts:3057-3087` handles ONLY `assembleCC`, logs "Unhandled postEffect type" for the rest ‚Äî so even the generic event system cannot boot a convention). `ConventionModal.tsx:22` is hardcoded "Philadelphia, {year}" (founding-flavored) | **`5c3a92a9` (Constitution Day, pman + Largo833, POST 1-5) ‚Äî the clearest forum statement of the mid-game ConCon as a DESIGNED, crisis-triggered path to re-found the government: (a) **TRIGGER** = the option unlocks "when Domestic Stab gets to its lowest spot" (Dom-Stab bottoming out, POST 1/3); (b) **per-state action + skill GATE** = an individual state's "call for a new Constitutional Convention" attempt FAILS if that state's Governor lacks 5 Gov (governing skill) (POST 4); (c) **THRESHOLD** = need 2/3 of the states to SUCCEED at the action (POST 4); (d) **RACE CONDITION** = must hit 2/3 BEFORE Dom-Stab rises by even a single point (recovery closes the window) ‚Äî "really hard" even with all ten factions trying, feasibility itself unresolved (POST 4/5); (e) **CPU DEFAULT** = CPUs back the historical option if no alternative passes, so a CPU-run convention reproduces the historical government / country ends "normal" (POST 3 ‚Äî a PARTIAL analog ships: `autoFillCPUVotes`+`preferredOption` `constitutionalConvention.ts:81-123` + the hardcoded `applyConvention` defaults `:136-144`, but only for the FOUNDING convention). PLUS a **scenario-boot-AT-a-ConCon** ask (POST 1/3): start a playtest DIRECTLY inside a mid-game convention at a crisis year (**1930 / Reconstruction ~1865-77 / 1968**) with Domestic Stability AND the Econ meter pre-set to WORST ‚Äî a start-anywhere boot the two hardcoded scenario builders do not support. Substrate is REUSABLE (`makeConvention`/`autoFillCPUVotes`/`applyConvention`/`ConventionModal`, founding-flavored) ‚Üí this is WIRING, not greenfield | (a) A Dom-Stab-lowest trigger that ENABLES the path (a new downstream consumer the `domestic` meter lacks); (b) a per-state "call a convention" gov-action with a 5-Gov skill-check (folds into the #20 gov-action library); (c) a 2/3-of-successful-states threshold raced against Dom-Stab recovery; (d) a mid-game invocation of `makeConvention`/`applyConvention` (de-found the "Philadelphia" UI copy) + port the CPU-historical-default auto-fill; (e) WIRE the dead `startConvention` postEffect (or equivalent) ‚Äî no mid-game convention has ANY wired trigger today; (f) a scenario-boot entry point that starts inside a `pendingConvention` with pre-set meters (Dom-Stab + Econ worst) at a chosen crisis year. Pairs with #20 (gov-action) / #92¬∑#115¬∑#186¬∑#228 (scenario-boot / start-anywhere cluster) / the Domestic-Stability meter. | 337 | **‚òÖ INDIAN-SOVEREIGNTY region STATUS ‚Äî a grantable status that turns a region into a foreign-country-like NON-PLAYER entity, OUT of the player's control (the INVERSE of #333 annexation-as-conquest [region JOINS] and a sibling of #334 sell/cede [region LEAVES for money]); a THIRD "region changes control" mechanic ‚Äî NEW (b63, `4c020c50`)** | **0% shipped ‚Äî no sovereignty mechanic of any kind.** grep `sovereignty\|Sovereignty\|tribal\|native\|Native` in `src/` finds NO region-status mechanic: all hits are unrelated ‚Äî **"popular sovereignty"** (`eraEvents1856.ts:19` Kansas; `factions1856.ts:6` Pop-Sov Democrats), **"state sovereignty"** (`phaseRunners.ts:3403`, an ideology-card string), Treaty-of-Paris flavor (`GameOverScreen.tsx:10`). `State.region` is a FIXED closed enum (`types.ts:1322`) with **NO** `Native`/`Sovereign`/`Tribal`/`Foreign` category ‚Äî a "this region is now a foreign-like non-player entity" status is UNREPRESENTABLE. So a region can ENTER a status whereby it stops being a player-owned/admittable state and behaves as a foreign nation (a diplomacy target outside player control). This is the **INVERSE of annexation** (#333: a foreign nation JOINS the union by conquest) and a SIBLING of the sell-a-state lever (#334: a state LEAVES the union voluntarily for a fiscal burst) ‚Äî together a triptych of "a region changes who controls it." UNOWNED as a mechanic: #333/#334 both cover the union GAINING or a state DEPARTING under player agency; NEITHER covers a region **becoming foreign-like / leaving player control while remaining on the map**. | 339 | **‚òÖ DRAFT VALUE = a STORED, hand-ranked prestige / PV score on the politician ‚Äî the authored draft-ordering input the shipped model RE-DERIVES rather than stores ‚Äî NEW (`politician-dataset`, `a960dbad`)** | **Partially ‚Äî the shipped model computes an equivalent but stores no authored value.** `computePV` (`pv.ts:67-89`) DERIVES a politician's PV at RUNTIME (skills√óoffice-weights √ó4 + `command`√ó10 + trait ¬±4/‚àí5 + `Kingmaker` +6 + office prestige + faction-leader +8 + age curve ‚àí flip-flopper penalty) and caches it as `pvCache` (`refreshPv` `:91-93`); PV drives draft-ordering and elections. But `Politician`/`ImportedDraftee` (`types.ts:~1780`) carry **NO stored `draftValue`/prestige field** ‚Äî nothing holds the designer's hand-ranked number; PV is always recomputed from the mechanical inputs | **The authoritative `politician-dataset` (source `a960dbad`, "Draft Value" column) is a STORED, hand-ranked prestige/electoral-weight score per politician: min **‚àí25**, p10 3, median 13, p90 30, **max 241** (Napoleon); 110 negatives. The marquee tier (Clay 140, Franklin 140, TR 139, Washington 120, Lincoln 118, FDR 114, Jackson 113, Jefferson 104, Calhoun 96) sits far above the long tail (one-term relatives 1-20) and joke/nobody entries at negatives (the DV ‚àí25 floor = the fictional "Duopoly Patine-Mambo"). Per the historian's grounding it is the roster's clearest editorial fingerprint ‚Äî the designer's DIRECT hand-ranking of historical weight/electability, used to order the DRAFT in the source game. This is an authored INPUT the shipped `computePV` re-derives from stats/traits/office instead of storing; the two coexist awkwardly (a marquee figure's authored DV and its computed PV need not agree). Either way reconcile the authored ‚àí25‚Ä¶241 scale against `computePV`'s `Math.max(0, ‚Ä¶)` non-negative output (the authored floor is negative; the shipped floor is 0). Pairs with #214 (the trait‚ÜíPV tiers that feed the derived PV) + #240 (the import that would carry the DV) + #338 (same authored file). OPEN (human): store-and-override vs derive-and-order-only; how to treat the negative-DV joke/nobody floor | ingested (**`politician-dataset` (source `a960dbad`, "Draft Value" column) ‚Äî min ‚àí25 / median 13 / p90 30 / max 241, 110 negatives (analysis.md ¬ßDraft Value; sample.md marquee DVs); historian grounding `historical-context-politician-roster.md` ("DV is the designer's hand-ranking‚Ä¶ the roster's clearest editorial fingerprint"); codebase-verified `computePV`/`refreshPv` (`pv.ts:67-93`) DERIVES PV at runtime (no stored authored value) and `Politician`/`ImportedDraftee` (`types.ts:~1780`) have no `draftValue` field. No prior owner (#214 = trait‚ÜíPV TIERS not a stored score; #240 = roster fill). OPEN: store-and-override vs derive-and-order-only; negative-floor handling. Pairs with #214/#240/#338**; **‚òÖ (`pol-additions-2024`, source `4b5d4ca7`) ‚Äî the ‚òÖ 182-column intended DATA MODEL SPLITS the score in TWO: it carries a **`Draft Value`** column (draft-time prestige) AND a distinct **`Historic Value`** column (`vstyle_clean.csv` cols 13-14) = the figure's *realized* weight ‚Äî the two-score refinement of this row's single stored Draft Value (many figures peaked below/above their promise; the historian ¬ßMethodology calls it "separates promise from realized weight"). Confirms the authored score is a first-class STORED field (both value columns are per-pol data, not runtime-derived), and adds the requirement to reconcile TWO authored scores, not one, against `computePV`'s single derived output. | 340 | **‚òÖ PER-RESPONSE "RESOLVE ABILITY" model on era-event responses ‚Äî each Pres Response A-F is resolved by an ABILITY CHECK (Admin unless stated) + a Modification, so success is skill-graded; the shipped `decider` picks WHO decides (an OFFICE), not HOW WELL ‚Äî NEW (`era-events` dataset, `b41ac9b1`)** | **0% shipped ‚Äî responses have no ability-resolution.** The shipped `EraEventResponse` (`types.ts:1459`) is `{ id, label, description, effect }` ‚Äî there is **no resolve/ability/modification field**; a response's effect applies flat, never gated on a skill roll. `EraEvent.decider` (`types.ts:1473`) is the OFFICE union `'president'\|'congress'\|'cabinet'\|'cc-president'\|'auto'` ‚Äî it chooses which office SELECTS the response, NOT an ability that resolves how successfully it lands. So the sheet's "Pres uses Judicial ability to break the Pullman Strike (with a Modification)" is unrepresentable ‚Äî there is no path from the response to the `Skills` scale (admin/legislative/judicial/military/governing/backroom, 0-5, `types.ts`) | **The authoritative era-event corpus (`b41ac9b1`, 2,475 events) gives every filled Pres Response A-F THREE resolution cells: a **Resolve** ability (canonically *"Admin unless stated otherwise"*, with per-response overrides ‚Äî Louisiana = a Pres/State/Ambassador modification, Pullman = Pres **Judicial**, Bleeding-Kansas = Pres **Governing**), a **Modification** (the modifier applied to the check), and a **Points** award (faction/ideology). So a response is a **skill-graded action**: the resolving office rolls its named ability (+ modifiers) ‚Üí success/partial/bungle drives the effect + the point sign (the sheet's `Pts (50-100-250, neg if bungled)` confirms a graded outcome). This is the era-event analogue of the war engine's per-action officer-skill roll (#56) and the investigation 6d6+modifier guilt roll (#330), but for the era-event response MENU. UNOWNED: no gap-log row covers ability-resolution of an era-event response; the shipped `decider`-as-office model (#169/#221) is the WHO, this is the HOW-WELL. ‚Üí minted NEW | Add a per-response **`resolveAbility`** (a `Skills` key, default `admin`) + **`modification`** field to `EraEventResponse`, and a resolution step that rolls the deciding office's ability (via the seeded `src/rng.ts`) + modifier to a graded success/bungle that selects the effect + point sign; keep DISTINCT from `EraEvent.decider` (the selecting OFFICE) and from the office-points effect (#341). | 341 | **‚òÖ OFFICE-POINTS effect channel + a 10%-next-ELECTION CARRY ‚Äî an era-event effect that grants/removes "office points" to a named office-HOLDER and carries a 10% chance of a ¬±1 election modifier for that officer next election; absent from the effect struct ‚Äî NEW (`era-events` dataset, `b41ac9b1`)** | **0% shipped ‚Äî neither half exists.** The shipped `EraEventResponseEffect` (`types.ts:1448`, also reused by `Legislation.effects`) can touch **meters / partyPreference / enthusiasm / interestGroups / domesticStability / diplomacy / startWar / text** ‚Äî there is **NO office-points field** (no per-office-holder point currency an event can move) and **NO next-election-carry** (no path from an event to a durable ¬±1 election modifier for a specific office-holder). `computePV`/PV (`pv.ts`) values politicians by skills√óoffice-weight etc., but nothing lets an EVENT award/dock a per-holder "office points" score, and elections read `state.bias` + `partyPreference` + `enthusiasm` ‚Äî never a carried per-officer event penalty | **The authoritative corpus (`b41ac9b1`) carries TWO dedicated effect columns ‚Äî **Immediate Office Points Gained** and **Immediate Office Points Lost** ‚Äî each headed *"(carries 10% chance of ¬±1 for the officer in next election)"*. The effect names an OFFICE (Pres / Gov of NY / Sec of State ‚Ä¶) that gains or loses office-points from the event (1,611 events carry a gained value; SpaceBotUSA docks `Pres` office-points), AND probabilistically carries a **10% chance of a ¬±1 election modifier for that office-holder next election** ‚Äî i.e. a bad event can dog an incumbent into the next race. DISTINCT from #292 (national election levers `partyPreference`/`enthusiasm`), from #335 (the presidential MANDATE win-state), and from #340 (the ability that RESOLVES a response; this is the EFFECT it can produce). | 346 | **‚òÖ Era-appropriate STATE roster as boot data (0‚Üí13‚Üí16‚Üí24‚Üí26‚Üí31‚Üí37‚Üí44‚Üí48‚Üí50) + the 2024-26 ~280-seat DATA HOLE (do NOT backfill) + deliberate game-scale compression ‚Äî NEW (dataset ingest `start-date-info`; the state-axis of #344; corroborates `admitState`/`expansionStates.ts` + #92)** | **State-admission system EXISTS but the per-window boot roster is not wired to a seed.** The engine has `admitState` (`territories.ts`) + `expansionStates.ts` for in-game admission, and the shipped scenarios hardcode their state set (1856 = 31-ish states). There is no boot mechanism that selects "for a 1820 boot, 24 states are present with their Gov/2 Sen/Reps; for 1892, 44" from a seed ‚Äî and no handling for a start-date column whose state rows are absent | **The `Start_Date_Info` state rows populate as states are admitted (historian-verified brackets in `historical-context-start-date-seeds.md`): 1772=0 (pre-Constitution) ¬∑ 1788=13 ¬∑ 1800=16 ¬∑ 1820=24 ¬∑ 1840=26 ¬∑ 1856=31 ¬∑ 1868=37 ¬∑ 1892=44 ¬∑ 1916-1948=48 ¬∑ 1972-2014=50. Corroborates the game's `admitState`/`expansionStates.ts` territory system and #92's territory-ownership content gating. So a boot at *D* must seat the era-correct STATE roster (Gov + 2 Sen + compressed Reps per present state) ‚Äî and NOT seat states not yet admitted. ‚òÖ TWO deliberate data facts downstream roles must NOT "correct": (1) **2024-26 is real-but-PARTIAL** ‚Äî only ~20/1021 seats filled (fed-exec only; **0 states, no Court/Congress/state rows = a ~280-seat HOLE**); flag as a DATA GAP, **do NOT infer or backfill** (per user directive). (2) **Structural compression is intentional** ‚Äî 148 House seats (not the real 435), 8 flag officers (not the full general/admiral corps), single ambassador per country, one snapshot per 2-year cycle: game-scale ABSTRACTIONS, not historical errors** | Wire the boot state-roster to a per-window seed (feeding #344): seat only admitted states with their compressed Gov/Sen/Rep rows, honoring the era count (0‚Üí50). Treat the 2024-26 column as INCOMPLETE ‚Äî a 2024 boot must either refuse until the column is completed or fall back to another source (OPEN, human); do NOT auto-backfill. Preserve the 148-House / 8-flag-officer / single-ambassador compression as-designed. This is the state-axis twin of #345. NO code this ingest ‚Äî data spec only. Pairs #92/#344/#345 + the `admitState`/`expansionStates.ts` system | ingested (**dataset `Start_Date_Info.xlsx` (dataset ingest ‚Äî no forum POST); digest `playtest-digests/start-date-info.md`; state-progression table in `docs/game/sources/start-date-info/analysis.md`; historian-verified admission brackets in `historical-context-start-date-seeds.md` (13/1788 ¬∑ 16/1800 incl VT/KY/TN ¬∑ 24/1820 Missouri-Compromise pair ¬∑ 31/1856 CA-31st ¬∑ 37/1868 NE-37th ¬∑ 44/1892 ¬∑ 48/1912 AZ-NM ¬∑ 50/1959 AK-HI); the 2024-26 ~280-seat hole + compression facts flagged as design-intent, do-not-backfill. Corroborates `admitState` (`territories.ts`) + `expansionStates.ts` + #92's territory-content gating**) |
| 347 | **‚òÖ Start-date SINGLE-SNAPSHOT turnover ‚Äî the seed picks ONE officeholder per 2-year window, but real history changed MID-WINDOW (Harrison‚ÜíTyler 31 days; Nixon‚ÜíAgnew‚ÜíFord two successions); design question: fixed-point snapshot vs scheduled succession event ‚Äî NEW (dataset ingest `start-date-info`, historian flags 1+2)** | **Not modeled.** No scenario in the build starts at 1840 or 1972, so this has never surfaced in shipped code; the shipped 1856 boot seats a single living President with no mid-cycle succession concept. There is no boot notion of "seed a president who is about to die/be-succeeded shortly into the cycle" | **The sheet must pick ONE holder per 2-year window, but two windows contain real mid-cycle successions the single seed cannot capture (historian flags 1+2, `historical-context-start-date-seeds.md`): ‚óÜ **1840-42 Harrison/Tyler** ‚Äî the seeded `President = W.H. Harrison` + `VP = Tyler` pairing was real for only **31 days** (Mar 4 ‚Äì Apr 4, 1841); Harrison died, Tyler succeeded (first VP succession, "His Accidency," left with no VP). ‚óÜ **1972-74 Nixon/Agnew** ‚Äî seeded `President = Nixon` + `VP = Agnew` was real only through **Oct 10, 1973**; the window then contains TWO successions (Agnew resigned‚ÜíFord confirmed Dec 1973; Nixon resigned Aug 9, 1974‚ÜíFord president). The seed captures NEITHER. These are DISTINCT from the routine inauguration-boundary picks (flag 3, folded into #344) ‚Äî those are a stable choice; these are a within-window CHANGE the single snapshot elides** | **DESIGN QUESTION (human):** for a window with a real mid-cycle succession, does the boot (a) seed the single living-President snapshot as a deliberate fixed-point simplification (what the sheet supports today), or (b) seed the snapshot AND schedule the succession (death/resignation) as a scripted era event firing shortly after boot (Harrison-death / Agnew-resign / Nixon-resign)? Option (b) makes 1840 a natural death-and-succession teaching scenario and 1972 a Watergate one. Only the single snapshot exists in the data; the succession would be authored era-event content (pairs the era-event corpus #340/#341 + the scripted-event registry #221). NO code this ingest ‚Äî flagged as an open design question. Pairs #344/#221/#340 | ingested (**dataset `Start_Date_Info.xlsx` (dataset ingest ‚Äî no forum POST); digest `playtest-digests/start-date-info.md`; historian flags 1 (Harrison/Tyler 31 days) + 2 (Nixon/Agnew, dual succession) in `historical-context-start-date-seeds.md` ¬ßA with real-date citations (Harrison d. Apr 4 1841; Agnew resigned Oct 10 1973; Nixon resigned Aug 9 1974); codebase-verified no mid-cycle-succession boot concept + no 1840/1972 scenario in `src/`. Distinct from the inauguration-boundary convention (flag 3, folded into #344). OPEN (human): fixed-point snapshot vs scheduled succession event**) |

## Confirmed shipped bugs

**Defects in existing behavior + design holes** (roadmap = fixes, not features).
`BUG-*` are shipped-code defects (surfaced by `fed`, verified against the codebase
where quick); `DH-*` are **GM-confirmed rules gaps / design holes** from the forum
where the rulebook had no answer (these are design defects to spec, not crashes).

| # | Bug | Symptom | Root cause / status | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BUG-1 | **Era events don't deactivate for non-1772/1856 start years** | A scenario whose **start year is past an era** still has that era's events in the roll table, *and* (per the official ruling) events deactivate **only** if the start year is past the era ‚Äî so e.g. an **1800-start game wrongly loses the Louisiana Purchase**. Stale events also dilute the roll table | The 1856 path calls `buildEraEventsForYear(year)` with **no era-vs-start-year filter** (phaseRunners.ts:2817). **Latent in the shipped build** (only 1772/1856 ship today) but a hard blocker the moment a federalism/1800 scenario is added. Wants an "era-lock" filter (unbuilt, fed posts 524, 535). Official ruling: `@Ich_bin_Tyler`, post 524 | ingested (fed 521-535; **rep1800 LIVE-CONFIRMS this exact bug: the GM found the Louisiana Purchase was listed under the prior Era of Federalism and so was ‚Äúnot included‚Äù for an 1800-start game; he hand-added it in 1816 ‚Äî verbatim ¬ßA POST 2668. Independent same-class confirmation; DH-30 is the companion event-scheduler-min-floor fix**) |
| BUG-2 | **`Chisholm v. Georgia` lacks an "11th Amendment not ratified" gate** | The SCOTUS case should be gated on the 11th Amendment not yet being ratified | **Case not yet in the build** ‚Äî no SCOTUS case data file contains it (grep: 0 hits in src). So this is a *forward requirement on the SCOTUS-case content*, not a live crash today. Logged to the dev's Basecamp by the forum | ingested (fed 145-150) |
| BUG-3 | **No fallback when there is no viable PM General candidate** | If no eligible politician exists for the PM General / `GeneralInChief` cabinet seat, the procedure is undefined ‚Üí potential crash (player hand-waved with a "Hand of God" stat bump) | `GeneralInChief` is a real seat (types.ts:1121) gated by `cabinetSeatsForYear` (types.ts:1196), but the no-candidate path isn't covered. **Reported, crash-path unverified** ‚Äî escalated to the dev (fed post 5) | ingested (fed 5, 119) |
| DH-1 | **A filibustered "MUST-pass" bill has no rules remedy** | The GM can declare a bill "MUST pass" (e.g. a required tariff each session); when the required tariff bill was **filibustered to death**, the rulebook had **no remedy**. The GM **improvised** a "special committee" of the 4 congressional leaders who must agree on a tariff that auto-passes, dropping EconStab + applying election penalties **per day until they agree** (an "AMPU government shutdown") | **GM-confirmed rules gap ‚Äî VERIFIED in-thread** (GM explicitly flagged it to fix). The shipped engine needs a defined rule for a failed required/MUST-pass bill (deadlock-resolution: forced compromise, shutdown penalty clock, or fallback auto-pass) | ingested (modern 640, 696, 711, 716) |
| DH-2 | **Modern era-event deck appears decoupled from the literal year** | In the ~2018 turn the EraEvos deck pulled **2008-era cards** (Subprime Mortgage Crisis, ABM/INF treaties) ‚Äî events fired years out of phase with the campaign clock | **Possible bug ‚Äî REPORTED, not verified.** Could be intended shuffle/backlog or a scheduling defect; reconcile with BUG-1 (era-event scheduling/era-lock) and the era-event scheduling-model open question | ingested (modern 2221) |
| DH-3 | **Career-track pols can run for President** | A career-track pol is barred from Gov/Rep/leadership/Kingmaker but **no rule bars a presidential run** | **GM-acknowledged rules gap, intended-to-fix, unbuilt.** Bar career-track pols from the presidential candidate pool (and from CPU presidential selection) | ingested (hd 8205-8219; relates to #63) |
| DH-4 | **"Extend Credit to all nations" near-auto-win foreign loop** | Diplomacy "Extend Credit" is per-nation | **Balance flag:** extending credit to all 7 powers "works amazingly every time" ‚Äî a positive trade balance with all nations triggers a stacked bonus (EconStab‚ÜíBooming + random industry + random foreign relation). Needs a diminishing-returns / cap rule | ingested (hd 7346; relates to #26/#27) |
| DH-5 | **Kingmaker-flip grabs the prot√©g√©(s) ‚Äî "insanely OP"** | Kingmaker/Prot√©g√© pairing exists (#design) | **Balance flag:** converting/flipping a Kingmaker also seizes his prot√©g√©s (and their +1 election standing) ‚Äî flagged "insanely OP." Needs a pairing-dissolution-on-flip rule (or no prot√©g√© transfer) | ingested (hd 7589, 8762; relates to #29/#30) |
| DH-6 | **Contingent-election top-2 vs top-3 deviation** | No contingent-election path | **House-rule deviation:** GM uses **top 2** vs the 12th Amendment's **top 3**, overruled "Game rules." Build must **pick a stated rule** | ingested (hd 5720-5721; see #62) |
| DH-7 | **R/D convention-threshold asymmetry + Iron-Fist unilateral change** | No convention thresholds | Dems run **3/4**, GOP **2/3‚Üí¬Ω+1** ‚Äî fixed era/party rule or temporary? Needs one canonical decision | ingested (hd 2274, 2279-2282, 3097-3098; relates to #22/#23) |
| DH-10 | **Blundered implementations still score (generic) unless a specific event overrides** | Implementation rolls exist (#22) | **Designer ruling (vcczar/MrPotatoTed/ebrk):** a FAILED (blundered) implementation **still scores points & moves meters "as if it succeeded"**; the blunder only risks scandal/resignation ‚Äî UNLESS a specific event/action overrides (does-not-pass / retriable). **Needs a per-action data flag + appendix** | ingested (hd 8649-8672; refines #22) |

Both need rebalancing | ingested (hd 7480-block, 7799; relates to #48/#5) |
| DH-12 | **White-peace rules MISSING from the war system** | War system not fully implemented (#45/#56) | **Designer-flagged:** Tyler (POSTS 6533-6541) ‚Äî *"There are, in fact, no rules about white peace"* ‚Äî had to dig up old treaty docs. | DH-13 | **Faithless-elector trigger over-aggressive + undocumented** | No faithless-elector roller | Generated whenever winner's state has the other party's ideology enthusiasm maxed (POST 466); kaldrich001 flagged historically unusual; 1876: 22 from Cobb, 12 from Fremont; 1892: 8 Whig (3 MA ‚Üí Clay, 5 NJ ‚Üí Shaw) created 232-232 tie ‚Üí House contingent; 1900: 8-elector defection. **EBR deadlock side-effect rule** (POST 5250): Controversial gain on elected Pres + 50/50 Dom Stab hit. **Needs documented + capped trigger** | ingested (drums 466, 469-471, 2912, 4441, 5250; refines #19) |
| DH-14 | **Bill ideology impacts not era-aware** (‚òÖ REFRAMED batch 29: the "never passes" is a CPU-AI / era-ideology-weighting artifact, NOT an absolute mechanical block) | Bills carry fixed ideology effects | Matt (POSTS 6691, 6878, 6912): Mods need to be removed from the negative-points side of Women's Suffrage in 1916 ‚Äî should be era-sensitive. Tyler agreed. **Equal Voting Rights for Women never passes** (60.5%, 63.6% short of 2/3); GM (all-CPU `drums`): *"This amendment will never pass in a game with CPUs."* Bills need era-keyed ideology impact tables. **‚òÖ REFRAME (`wilsons1916`, batch 29):** in a **human-majority 1916 game** the SAME "Equal Voting Rights for Women Amendment" **PASSES comfortably** ‚Äî House **374-61**, Senate **79-17**, then ratified by states (VT the 36th, P383) ‚Üí so the suffrage block is NOT a hard mechanical wall; it is a **CPU-vote-modeling + era-keyed-bill-ideology weighting** problem (the all-CPU `drums` field couldn't muster the 2/3 because Moderates sit on the negative side AND the CPU vote model under-supports it; humans with the same era mix pass it). The fix is therefore TWO things: (1) the era-keyed bill-ideology impact table (move Mods off the suffrage negative side in 1916, the original DH-14 ask), AND (2) the CPU vote model for amendments must be able to reach a 2/3 the way a human field does (`GM‚áíApp`, pairs with #114/E9/K5 + #208) | ingested (drums 6691, 6878, 6912; **‚òÖ wilsons1916 P383 ‚Äî 19th Amendment RATIFIED in a 10-human 1916 game (House 374-61 / Sen 79-17), contradicting the `drums` all-CPU "never passes"; reframes DH-14 as a CPU-AI/era-weighting artifact**; **‚òÖ (b56) `92202e9a` ("Jeannette Rankin situation?", Feb 2024) supplies the DESIGN-INTENT for what the amendment's PASSAGE GATES ‚Äî i.e. WHY it matters mechanically: its passage UNLOCKS female candidacy (vcczar POST 2, "that amendment has to be passed" before a woman can run/hold office). This row + the 1916 digests record the amendment EXISTS as a fireable event + bill (passes in human games); this thread records its CONSEQUENCE (the eligibility gate it flips, #238). ‚òÖ In `src/data` the suffrage event/bill is ABSENT (grep of `src/data` = no suffrage event/bill), though `EraEvent`/`Legislation` machinery exists to author one; the female-candidacy gate it would flip is also unbuilt (#238 ‚Äî no gender attribute). Cross-ref #238 (the gender attribute + per-state-suffrage option) / #258 (the per-state-suffrage predicate class) / #114 (solo-app intent). `92202e9a` POST 2**) |

Roosevelt's Internationalist + Pro-Federal-Government + Advocate New Freedoms triple-stack tanked all meters with no AI penalty. **No mechanism penalizes the CPU AI for choosing terrible-for-meters scripted event options**, and no AI logic considers whether stacking events on a single meter could push it to crisis | ingested (drums 6280; refines #74/#75) |

Tyler called it *"A lot of events and unfortunate rolls."* Cabinet replacement chain resolves 3 deep in one event (Rep ‚Üí VP ‚Üí President ‚Üí new VP from Senate, each triggering state-Gov replacement chains). **No smoothing for "back-to-back at-most-once" event types** | ingested (drums 7389; affects all era-event subsystems) |
| DH-23 | **Cabinet 50/50 Admin-1 reject + lobby-maximizing selection = 36% pass-rate bug** | Cabinet phase exists (#25/#31/#73) | The two CPU bugs reinforce: **(a) Admin-1 nominees roll 50/50 in committee** (Whig minority auto-NAY + 50/50 Dem NAY = guaranteed fail); **(b) lobby-maximizing selection rule picks Admin-nobodies who hold the right cards** rather than high-Admin competents. **Combined cause of designer-acknowledged 36% confirmation pass rate** (POSTS 4702-4708 ‚Äî vcczar: "low chance to reject" rule was lost from rules doc). | DH-24 | **Senate-class boot data buggy for fresh modern scenarios** | Modern scenario not in build; no senate-class data verification | When a fresh modern scenario is set up from a pre-built sheet, **Senate class assignments are wrong in the seed data** (`pop` POST 272, 297, 298): Ron Johnson (WI) was up in 2010 not 2012; GM had to swap classes mid-election. **Boot-data quality is a real problem** when assembling a modern boot from a real-history snapshot ‚Äî the build needs a Senate-class verifier that checks each senator's last-election year against the class. Pairs with #86. **`dem1820` corroborates from a 2nd boot:** the GA ran the entire 1822 midterm as **Class I** then discovered it should be **Class II**, forcing a full re-submission of every Senate appointment (*"Oh for fuck's sake‚Ä¶ I love this game a lot and I also really hate it"*, POST 758). A Senate-class verifier is now a 2-thread requirement | ingested (pop 272, 297, 298; **dem1820 POST 747-758**; relates to #86) |
| DH-25 | **Career-track bootstrap unresolved (3-yr-stale design discussion)** | Career-track field exists (types.ts); no bootstrap rule for mid-game scenarios | Lars (`pop` POST 33): *"we've legit been having this discussion for almost three years now and somehow we've yet to change the rulebook or the game."* For mid-game boots the question is "which existing pols start on career tracks at boot?" Zagnut's houserule: anyone drafted in 1996+ goes onto one track each (POST 31). Rodja hand-populated by GM ad-hoc (POST 38, 50). **3-year-stale unresolved design hole ‚Äî needs a stated rule before the modern scenario ships.** **`dem1820` adds the inaugural-draft seeding rule AND a fresh contradiction:** the stated rule is "**at the inaugural draft you may put rookies from the LAST 3 DRAFT CLASSES onto the career track**" (1820/1816/1812/1808; POST 28) ‚Äî but 2.1 *also* says CT pols "will be unavailable to run in elections / to be appointed," which **contradicts the many sections that let CT pols be pulled by election/appointment**; GA ruling: *"the rules on this definitely need work‚Ä¶ we'll ignore [it] because it isn't consistently applied"* (POST 850, 859). So the build must pick ONE coherent CT-eligibility rule (corroborates DH-56) | ingested (pop 31, 33, 38, 50; **dem1820 POST 28, 32, 850, 859**; **terror2000 ‚Äî 3rd-thread corroboration of the SAME hole at a non-1772 boot: "stuck with 1-skill Admin/Mil/Judicial pols incapable of doing anything ‚Ä¶ a problem starting in any era except Independence" (POST 63); ebrk's improvised fix = seed ~2 pols per track type per faction at boot (POST 63-65); existing CT pols already seeded + only rookies addable to tracks (POST 182-183)**; relates to #86 + DH-56; **‚òÖ (b45) CONCRETE 20-yr exit-YIELD example + the FORCED-EXIT CAP (`0b896cf9` June-2023 POST 24, 1840 run) ‚Äî distinct from this row's boot-SEEDING: the stated rule is "after 20 years, politicians have to come out of the Career Track." William "Boss" Tweed (drafted 1848, Backroom Track, pulled at the 1868 20-yr cap) exited with NINE traits ‚Äî Leadership, Kingmaker, Admin +1, Education, Expansionist, Charisma, Orator, Teflon, Unlikable ‚Äî PLUS a manual ideology shift MOD‚ÜíCONS. The observed yield is far BROADER than the shipped `TRACK_THEMED_TRAITS.Backroom = ['Manipulative','Kingmaker','Numberfudger']` (`src/types.ts:212`) and includes a NEGATIVE trait (Unlikable) + a STAT bump (Admin +1) + an explicit 20-yr FORCED-EXIT cap not encoded in `TRACK_THEMED_TRAITS`/`CAREER_TRACK_CAP`. So the exit gain-pool and the forced-exit timing are richer than the shipped themed-traits list. Downstream snowball it enabled: Congress in the 1872 wave despite Unlikable ‚Üí freshman Speaker ‚Üí faction head (+1 Command) ‚Üí convention keynote (+1 next national cycle, see #40/keynote) ‚Üí 1876 presidential contender (assassinated, see #283). OPEN: is the broad trait set random-from-pool or track-weighted, and what governs negative-trait draws? `0b896cf9` POST 24**) |
| DH-26 | **3rd-party VP "same traits" rule is prohibitive** | 3rd-party trigger is `modern`-only design (#48); no VP-trait gating | The rule for an independent presidential run requires *"a VP with the same traits"* as the presidential candidate, making 3rd-party tickets **nearly impossible** in practice (Lars `pop` POST 945-947). Pairs with DH-11 (Dem 3rd-party structural bias). **Both 3rd-party design holes need addressing simultaneously** ‚Äî relax the trait constraint AND fix the structural bias | ingested (pop 945-947; relates to #48 / DH-11) |
| DH-27 | **Trait-conflict adjudication slips in boot/draft data** | `TRAIT_CONFLICTS` exists (types.ts:658) but is run only on trait ADD events | `pop` POST 1139: **Quinn has both Integrity AND Controversial simultaneously** ‚Äî explicitly conflicting traits, GM doesn't resolve in chunk. The trait-conflict table prevents acquiring a conflicting trait via the normal gain events, but **boot/draft seed data can slip conflicting traits through** because the conflict check isn't run on dataset import. Build TODO: validate `TRAIT_CONFLICTS` against the standard-draft-classes.json at dataset-load time + at scenario-boot time. **`dem1820` corroborates:** the conflict check is not native to the sheet ‚Äî the GA had to hand-build conditional formatting for "**3.0.34**" trait conflicts across all 10 player tabs (POST 577, 596), and a separate dataset bug rendered every pol Black/LGBT from a column mismatch (POST 104, 125 ‚Äî see DH-53/the demographic-corruption note) | ingested (pop 1139; **dem1820 POST 104, 125, 577, 596**; relates to #86 + types.ts:658) |
| DH-28 | **"Repeal deals with climate crisis" tag ‚Äî incomplete data tagging is gameable** | No `bill.tags[]` field surveyed | Only some Repeal bills carry the "deals with climate crisis" tag (`pop` POST 552): *"Not sure why everything that can impact the meter doesn't have that attribute, but hey, that's for the Plan B devs."* **Players gamed this** ‚Äî bills that should affect Planet Health but aren't tagged slip through scoring. Build TODO: per-bill meter-impact tags must be **complete and verified** at dataset-build time ‚Äî incomplete tagging is a gameable balance hole. Generalizes to other meter-impact tags | ingested (pop 552; relates to #87) |
| DH-29 | **Reconstruction Strict/Ironclad plan can essentially NEVER pass with CPU ‚Üí solo Reconstruction unresolvable** | Reconstruction not in build (#57) | **‚òÖ Headline solo-play blocker (GM-VERIFIED).** `rep1800` POST 9170: *"the CPU factions will never vote for that bill‚Ä¶ including red, only 3 factions would ever consider voting for it. A real problem when it is the historical plan passed and **in a single player game it basically can never pass.**"* Both readmission plans (Strict-Ironclad AND, post-guerrilla-war, even Lenient-10%) were effectively un-passable solo. GM names this a **primary driver of the in-progress CW/Reconstruction rules rewrite.** Any single-player antebellum/CW scenario inherits this ‚Äî the historical-plan bill needs a solo-passable path (CPU default-vote bias for the historical plan, or a guaranteed-pass crisis path, or a re-spec). **Flag prominently for the roadmap.** **‚òÖ‚òÖ `hd1` MOVEMENT (human-played confirmation + the FIX):** in the Part-1 run with **HUMANS on BOTH sides** of the Reconstruction tug-of-war (Southern-Dem wanting the 10% plan vs Radical-Rep wanting Ironclad/Mass-Trials), **the choice STILL deadlocked ‚Äî neither plan passed and states drifted back into the Union with NO plan at all** ("The Congress could never agree on a Reconstruction guideline‚Ä¶ in the end, neither passed," POST 2678). So the blocker is **structural, not a CPU-only artifact** (competing humans filibustered each other to the same null result). **The FIX is now designed (vcczar, #156):** ALL readmissions require **"a plan adopted by Congress OR by the President"** ‚Äî the President can **unilaterally adopt** a plan, guaranteeing resolution in both solo and deadlocked-human play (POST 2693). **Build must implement the President-can-adopt-unilaterally rule.** | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßC 9166, 9170; **hd1 POST 2678, 2692-2694**; refines #57; FIX = #156) |
| DH-30 | **Era-event scheduler has a MAX per half-term but NO MIN floor** | Era-event scheduling not min-bounded (relates to BUG-1) | `rep1800` POST 2919‚Äì2932: events are scaled by a fixed NUMBER per era (over a 20-yr era a 25%-event ‚âà94% cumulative, 10%‚Üí65%, 5%‚Üí40%), but "the limit is a max not a min‚Ä¶ which isn't what we discussed." **Agreed fix: minimum = 20% of the era's max (round down)**; if still none fire (all prereq-gated), fall back to the current 5 generic anytime events. A rejected alt (hard year-windows per event) was declined because *"events not happening every playthrough has been explicitly stated to be how the game operates"* (load-bearing for alt-history). **Companion to BUG-1.** | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßA 2918, 2919-2932; companion to BUG-1) |
| DH-31 | **Procedure-subtype bills BYPASS the veto but the engine MIS-ROUTES them to the President** | Bill `subtype` taxonomy / veto routing not surveyed | `rep1800` POST 2342‚Äì2348: buried-in-rules `subtype: procedure` bills (Institute Filibuster, create-whip-offices) should **not** go to the President for sign/veto, but the engine routes them there. (Corroborates the `hd`-class procedure-routing flag.) | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßA 2342-2348; relates to #42 bill-typing) |
| DH-32 | **SCOTUS can void a STATE ‚Üí "a state cannot be ruled unconstitutional"** | SCOTUS docket not in build (#52); no state-existence guard | `rep1800`: "Pickens v. Maine's Existence" passed **5-1** ‚Üí Maine statehood **voided AFTER a census had counted it**. Consensus fix: add a rule that **a state cannot be ruled unconstitutional** (a territory can be revoked; secession is the only un-making of a state). Needs a guard in the SCOTUS ruling-effect path. | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßA 2180-2182 [principle]; ¬ßB 3632, 3646-3652; refines #52) |
| DH-33 | **Impeachment ruleset "outdated, doesn't work"** | Impeachment path partially in build; canonical rules flagged broken | `rep1800` ¬ßA 465‚Äì474 / ¬ßB 3594, 3620: the impeachment mini-flow runs but the canonical rules are flagged outdated/non-functional and improvised (an Integrity justice got accused of bribery ‚Äî nonsensical; only **resignation** avoids the DomStab/Honest-Gov drop). Corroborates `hd` DH-14 (impeachment "super outdated and doesn't work") across a 2nd campaign ‚Äî a real rewrite is needed. **‚òÖ (b43) NOW CONSOLIDATED UNDER #273** ‚Äî the `ae435b5f` "Fourteen Points on Impeachment" thread supplies the precise ROOT-CAUSE for this row (POST 1-2: the rule predates the 3‚Üí25-member House-committee revamp, so "every committee member picks a qualifying offense" yields ~25 articles; "qualifying offense" undefined). This row is KEPT as a corroborating broken-3.05 FLAG; the end-to-end rewrite/spec lives at #273 (tagged DESIGN-INTENT, NOT designer-ratified ‚Äî vcczar deferred POST 47/48). | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßA 465-474; ¬ßB 3594, 3620; corroborates hd DH-14; **‚òÖ (b43) root-cause + consolidated spec at #273 ‚Äî `ae435b5f` POST 1-2**) |
| DH-34 | **Static era-biases are unfixable ("AMPU 2.0") ‚Üí the Red-unwinnable hole** | `State.bias` is a static per-era table; no policy-reactive bias | The single most-repeated `rep1800` complaint: state party-biases don't react to policy (abolish slavery ‚Üí South should swing Red), so the **Federalists are acknowledged-unwinnable 1800‚Äì1840** (Blue 142-0 House by 1804; Jefferson 179-0/197-15) and players quit. GM + Willthescout7 confirm **dynamic/policy-reactive biases are "too complicated / not part of the AMPU vision‚Ä¶ maybe AMPU 2.0."** Roadmap decision: ship static biases (accept the imbalance) vs. invest in policy-reactive biases. Pairs with #21/#34 (bias as a field) + DH-29's solo-balance theme. | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßA 22, 350, 720-747, 1328-1335, 2444-2457, 2641, 2711-2713) |
| DH-35 | **Thin early-era presidential agency ("this era is a bore")** | Exec-action library is era-flat (#23) | `rep1800` ¬ßA 2756‚Äì2760, 2930, 3110: in the pre-primary eras the President's only exec actions are flavor tours ‚Äî the modern toolkit (primaries, rich exec actions) isn't unlocked yet, feeding the "Blue auto-wins, governing is dull" sentiment. The exec-action / primary libraries must be **era-gated with enough EARLY-era agency** to keep the pre-convention eras engaging (pairs with #23 exec-action library + #63 primary-era unlock). | ingested (rep1800 ¬ßA 2756-2760, 2930, 3110; relates to #23/#63) |
| DH-36 | **‚òÖ Manual spreadsheet bookkeeping caused GM BURNOUT that KILLED a 12-turn game** | Forum is run in Google Sheets w/ manual dice; the build owns state | **‚òÖ The single strongest cross-cutting case that the build must own ALL upkeep/bookkeeping.** `new1772` (the first MP 1772) was **ABANDONED mid-campaign** ‚Äî not lost, not won ‚Äî because **the GM ran out of steam** (POST 3607 verbatim: *"the simple truth is that while the game has been fun to run, it's also been a very tiring process and I've run out of steam‚Ä¶ I just can't do it."*). No successor GM stepped up; play stopped at ~12 turns before President-elect Washington took a single in-office action. The thread is also riddled with the proximate causes: career-track flags left stale after a pol was pulled (missed gains, retro-fixes, "no retcon once a turn is missed"), formula breakage from editing the Master tab, hand-cleaning LegisActive/LegisProp tabs of 2 centuries of irrelevant default laws. **Hard product mandate:** the build must auto-manage career-track state, filter active legislation/lobbies by era, and remove all manual ledger upkeep ‚Äî the bookkeeping load is an existential threat to long campaigns, not a UX nicety. (Reinforces A9 House-slate/committee-persistence + the "forum drives the build" theme) **‚òÖ SECOND burnout death (`dem1820`, batch 10):** GA @MrPotatoTed ended the 1820 playtest at ~1822-23 (POST 900) ‚Äî *"these used to be a lot of fun to run and now it just feels argumentative"* ‚Äî driven by **player friction over undocumented/inconsistent rules** (the strip-Command ruling, the House-incumbency argument, the career-track-eligibility flip, "an incredibly insulting message") COMPOUNDED by relentless manual bookkeeping (a 10-page / 8-day midterm, constant sheet repair). Tell: *"I'm currently helping run an all-CPU game for a reason"* (POST 905). So **2 of the KB's threads were killed by GM burnout, not by a loss/win** ‚Äî both reinforce: the build must own ALL upkeep AND have a coherent, disclosed ruleset (#115). **‚òÖ TWO MORE burnout deaths (batch 24, both 1772):** `grass1772` (a 2-human game) DIED specifically of the **CPU-faction-management load** ‚Äî "didn't have the time to run the two player factions AND all 8 CPU factions" (POST 328), and the relaunched 8-human version then petered out at setup ("We still playing this?", POST 544); `rookie1772` DIED of **GM time-burnout** ("found myself without the time to properly continue to GM this game" + an international trip, no successor, POST 1444) after the solo run had already proven unsustainable ("running this by myself feels like a part time job," POST 36). **So 4 of the KB's threads were killed by GM/upkeep burnout, not by a loss/win** ‚Äî and `grass1772` adds the sharpest angle yet: the burnout was driven by the **manual CPU simulation** specifically (ties to #114). | ingested (**new1772 ¬ß7.24 + ¬ß0 / POST 311-312, 439, 957, 963, 3607-3614**; **dem1820 POST 900, 902-905; rules-friction at POST 79-97, 665-696, 850-859**; **grass1772 POST 328, 348, 544; rookie1772 POST 36, 1444**; **‚òÖ summer2021 ‚Äî the largest run in the corpus [9012 posts / 14 months] needed a 3-GM RELAY [vcczar‚ÜíMrPotatoTed‚ÜíRezi] to survive that long, with persistent burnout-adjacent delays from hand-simming up to 5 CPU factions/party + per-package multi-chamber sway votes + the Const-Convention machine + cabinet lingering rolls + the federalism decision/diplomacy spine; it did NOT formally burnout-quit but simply TRAILED OFF mid-1808-10 floor-vote ‚Äî a 5th GM-upkeep-driven death, this time by attrition rather than a declared quit; reinforces: the build must own ALL upkeep**; **‚òÖ batch 32 `georgemartha` ‚Äî a SOLO dice-roller's clean confirmation that even a ONE-person game offloads the mechanical per-turn work: Lars refuses to narrate every roll ("I won't report every single little roll") and built a reused **"Lars' Death and Retirements Rolling Python Script ‚Äî rolls everything 200x faster"** to automate the death/retirement pass (POST 1, 24, 34, 43, 63) ‚Äî the SAME script later reused in `arkzag` (a recurring community automation). The death/retirement roller is exactly the pure-math engine subsystem the build must own (#130 schedule); corroborates that the solo founding game STILL scripts death/retirement by hand-tool, the load #114/DH-36 says the app must absorb. georgemartha (0500fa72) POST 1, 24, 34, 43, 63; pairs with #114/#130**; **‚òÖ (b43) the DESIGNER says it plainly + a fresh manual-sim corroboration: `ddd179cb` (playtester survey) MrPotatoTed Q10 "I'd make it into a computer game so I didn't have to CPU all the time" + "Processing Lingering" voted the most time-consuming/boring GA chore (matthewyoung123 + jnewt, Q8) ‚Äî the load-bearing argument that the app must own all upkeep; `06fbb2e5` (Lars-Ark 1772) shows the proximate bookkeeping friction live (mis-run proposer order POST 33/36, bad eligibility picks POST 64, a transient CPU 2.1.5-relocate failure POST 20). `ddd179cb` Q8/Q10 POST 11, 14, 20; `06fbb2e5` POST 20, 33, 36, 64**) |

| DH-38 | **No late-ratification / "Rogue Island" / abstaining-state path; amendments are one-time votes with no multi-year window** | Amendments treated as durable (#39); ratification is a single vote (#39/#64) | **Top AMPU-2 gap (`new1772` design-hole #8).** States **can't ratify late, abstain, or be embargoed** ‚Äî CT & MD declined to ratify the Constitution but were **pulled in anyway** because excluding them needs election/representation rules that don't exist. There is **no multi-year amendment-ratification window** (compare `rep1800`/`tea1772`, where stragglers CAN ratify later in the Governor phase ‚Äî #39; so the design is inconsistent and the founding-era ratification is the unmodeled one). Decide a stated late-ratification/abstention model + a multi-year ratification window | ingested (**new1772 ¬ß7.8 / POST 1844, 1958, 1972**) |

The build needs a defined **human-deadlock resolution** for the Convention (and likely for any all-human core vote that can't reach quorum) | ingested (**new1772 ¬ß7.7 / POST 1808, 1824**) |

Pennsylvania** should logically void/neuter the Fugitive Slave Act, but the engine **doesn't cascade** a ruling onto contradictory laws on the books ‚Äî *"I argued that but was told that **V [vcczar] will need to think about it**"* (`tea1772` POST 124-126). | DH-74 | **‚òÖ Legislation-proposal list is NOT validated against current game state ‚Üí bills proposed/voted/packaged, then found INVALID mid-vote ‚Üí wasted votes + GM phase-restarts ‚Äî NEW (`wilsons1916`, `GM‚áíApp`; the Progressive face of DH-60 for LEGISLATION)** | DH-60 captures era-prereq gating for EVENTS; there is no equivalent state-validation of the BILL proposal list against active territories / ratified amendments / "recent war" predicates / era prerequisites before a bill can be proposed and voted | **The single biggest PROCESS failure in `wilsons1916`.** Bills were proposed, voted through committee, packaged, and House-voted ‚Äî then found INVALID against game state mid-second-vote: **P303** ‚Äî *"Hawaii territory is legis active, so I won't move it to the Senate / **Teller Amendment is active, so we can't make Cuba a client-state** / Spanish-American war is not recent so we can't issue pensions‚Ä¶ sighs this is what happens when legis props change in the middle of things."* The GM **HALTED and restarted phase 2.6** (P311-313) and players had to replace **3 already-voted bills**; it recurred (P337 Hudson-waterway "another proposal-that-should-not-have-been"), with players asking *"why are so many things in the proposal list that we can't propose?"* (P338). So the proposal list offered bills that were illegal given **active territories, already-ratified amendments, era prerequisites, and "recent war" predicates** ‚Äî there is no pre-proposal state-validation, producing wasted committee/floor votes and two manual phase restarts. The engine already owns this state ‚Äî surface only legal proposals so no committee/floor vote is wasted and no phase needs a manual restart. Pairs with DH-60/#92/BUG-1/#11 | ingested (**wilsons1916 ch4 P303, P311-313, P337-338**; **‚òÖ (b50) the MUTUALLY-EXCLUSIVE / CONFLICTING-BILL case of this validation gap, surfaced live (`59d805be` "Hand of Providence," all-CPU 1772, POST 71): **two conflicting "Move Capital" bills (NY and Boston) both PASSED the same session** with no defined resolution ‚Äî the GM ad-hoc'd a tiebreak ("pick the one with fewer No votes") and flagged "we probably should have this figured out for when two conflicting bills pass, or maybe it is there and I just don't see it." So beyond pre-proposal validation (the wilsons1916 face), the legislation pipeline also lacks **conflict/supersession resolution when two mutually-exclusive bills BOTH pass** ‚Äî needs an engine rule (one supersedes, or they can't both reach the floor). The FAQ corroborates that "Move Capital" is a real repeatable legislative proposal (multiple capital options; moving to DC FINALIZES it, `58da1636` POST 76-85). Pairs with DH-60/#92/#11; extends this row's scope from proposal-validation to post-passage conflict resolution. `59d805be` POST 71; `58da1636` POST 76-85**) |

| DH-76 | **‚òÖ Senate has a filibuster but NO cloture mechanic + NO CPU filibuster-override rule ‚Üí a filibustered bill/nominee is simply delayed a full term ‚Äî NEW (`wilsons1916` + `welcome2future`, `GM‚áíApp`)** | The forum loop includes a 2.6.1 "filibuster/cloture" step (#110) but the cloture half is unspecified; there is no rule for invoking cloture and no rule for whether a CPU leader overrides a filibuster | **A designed rules gap surfaced from BOTH new threads.** `wilsons1916` P349: *"No rules to invoke cloture exist, so any bill filibustered will be delayed until 1918-20"* ‚Äî Puritan senators filibustered (hard-drugs criminalization + an income-tax change were killed for the whole term) and there was **no cloture path to break it**. `welcome2future` confirms the CPU side: CPU senators with the right profile DO filibuster nominees (Mike Lee killed Sullivan+Locke; Cruz killed Solis; filibustered pols gained `Controversial`), and the GM flagged **there is NO rule for whether a CPU Majority Leader overrides a filibuster** (improvised "won't override own party unless it targets his faction," posts 331, 343). (The era-keyed confirmation thresholds + Nuclear-Option toggle in #172 are the *cabinet/SCOTUS-confirm* analog; this is the *legislative* filibuster/cloture gap.) **‚òÖ ORIGIN (batch 30): the filibuster+cloture rule was FIRST AUTHORED in `summer2021` (Constructive-Results changelog) ‚Äî POST 98: cloture invoked by the Sen-Maj-Leader to beat a filibuster, failed cloture delays the bill/package a half-term, ONE filibuster per half-term (TWO if the filibusterer is Efficient), filibuster only by a Senate officer/committee member, both are procedure bills, Senate default = simple majority; POST 129 amendment: a "Puritan" may filibuster more than once if also "disharmonious."** So `wilsons1916`'s "no cloture rule exists" is the field re-discovering that the `summer2021`-authored cloture half was never carried into the build. Pairs with #110 (the 2.6.1 step), #172 (confirm thresholds / Nuclear-Option), #208 (CPU legislative logic) | Add a cloture mechanic to the Senate filibuster (the `summer2021` spec: maj-leader-invoked, failed cloture = half-term delay, 1/half-term, 2 if Efficient, Puritan+Disharmonious extra; era-keyed threshold tied to the Nuclear-Option state where relevant) so a filibuster is breakable rather than an automatic full-term delay; specify the CPU maj-leader filibuster-override decision (folds into #208). Pairs with #110/#172/#208 | ingested (**wilsons1916 ch5 P349; welcome2future posts 331, 343; ‚òÖ ORIGIN summer2021 POST 98, 129**; **‚òÖ SHARPENED-AUTHORITATIVE (batch 31, `rulebook` ¬ßV POST 26 + ¬ßM2 POST 16): the rulebook gives the canonical CLOTURE half DH-76 found missing ‚Äî at committee review (2.6.2) the Majority-of-Majority rule lets a Speaker/SML w/ Manipulative block one opposing-party committee bill; the cabinet-confirmation filibuster (a committee-relevant Puritan Senator) is broken by INVOKE CLOTURE = SML w/ Manipulation + higher Legislative than the filibusterer forces a vote. So cloture is gated on the SML's Manipulation + a Legislative-advantage over the filibusterer, NOT a flat supermajority roll ‚Äî a deterministic gate, matching the summer2021 origin. rulebook ¬ßV/¬ßM2 POST 16, 26**) |
| DH-77 | **‚òÖ `pv.ts` ships a PRE-REVAMP Politician-Value formula ‚Äî flat ¬±4/‚àí5 per trait, flat `command √ó 10`, magic `Kingmaker +6`, and a `Math.max(0,‚Ä¶)` clamp that destroys ordering among sub-zero pols ‚Äî NEW (codebase; the defect counterpart of design gap #214/#215/#220)** | `computePV` (`pv.ts`): traits resolve to `+4` (any positive) / `‚àí5` (any negative) (`pv.ts:76-77`); skills are flat `skill √ó officeWeight √ó 4` (`pv.ts:70-73`); `command √ó 10` flat per level (`pv.ts:74`); `Kingmaker` adds a hard-coded `+6` (`pv.ts:79`); the result is `Math.max(0, ‚Ä¶)`-clamped (`pv.ts:88`) | **The shipped reality the trait‚ÜíPV revamp supersedes ‚Äî recorded as a defect so the four specific code sites are traceable.** All four are explicitly superseded by the `revampPV` design: the flat ¬±4/‚àí5 ‚Üí per-trait tiers (#214); the flat skill/command weighting ‚Üí the first-point-worth-more curve + Command ‚âà25/level (#215); the magic `Kingmaker +6` ‚Üí table-driven Kingmaker/Master-Kingmaker (#214); the hard clamp ‚Üí a constant floor-offset so the CPU keeps ordering among bad pols (#220). The clamp is the most clearly *wrong* today: it floors negative PV to 0, collapsing the ordering of below-average pols that the CPU's draft/leadership heuristic still needs. **NOT a quick fix** ‚Äî the replacement is the whole #214/#215/#216/#220 cluster (a re-fit, a name remap, and a display decision), so this row is the defect anchor, not an independent patch. Pairs with #214/#215/#216/#220 | codebase (pv.ts:70-88 ‚Äî flat ¬±4/‚àí5 traits, flat skill+command weighting, Kingmaker +6, Math.max(0) clamp; superseded by `revampPV` design, see #214); **‚òÖ BATCH-33 RE-SCOPE (`groupthinkpv` c6f6dcc7 resolves the lackey confusion): the famous **"lackey ‚àí53" was a forum-SPREADSHEET TYPO**, ruled down to **‚àí5** (POST 22-26, MPT: "Yep. ‚àí5"). Shipped `pv.ts:76-77` already resolves lackey (a negative trait) to exactly **‚àí5** ‚Äî so `pv.ts` is ALREADY CORRECT on lackey; there is NO lackey bug in code. This row is therefore NOT a "lackey ‚àí53 defect" ‚Äî it is the **pre-revamp flat ¬±4/‚àí5 + command√ó10 + Kingmaker +6 + clamp** state, superseded by the #214/#215/#220 cluster. **Worst PENALTY trait = Incompetent (‚àí20, the "career killer"), NOT lackey** (POST 7/15/20-21); lackey "rarely comes up‚Ä¶ not that bad" (POST 3, 22) and its real downside is mechanical lock-in (see #214 EFFECTS layer). groupthinkpv POST 3, 7, 15, 20-26**) |

| DH-79 | **‚òÖ Naval battle results are RECORDED but NEVER consulted in the war win/loss check ‚Äî the "one naval win ends naval forever" bug, confirmed in shipped code ‚Äî NEW (`rethinkwar`; codebase-verified)** | `revolutionaryWar.ts` records `war.navalWins++/war.navalLosses++` after each naval engagement (`:202`), but the war-end check (`:254-264`) tests ONLY `war.currentGroundWins >= war.groundWinsNeeded` (=7) / `war.currentGroundLosses >= war.groundLossesRemaining` (=16, gated by `!war.frenchAlliance`) ‚Äî naval tallies feed nothing | **The OP's complaint, confirmed in code.** MrPotatoTed (`rethinkwar` POST 1): *"winning one naval battle means you no longer have to worry about naval at all, even if the land invasion continues for another ten years."* Verified: the shipped 1772 engine fights at most ONE naval battle per phase (`:186-204`), increments `navalWins`/`navalLosses` (`:202`), but the win/loss decision (`:254-264`) is purely ground-count-based ‚Äî so naval outcomes have **no bearing on whether the war ends**, naval never "continues to matter," and the French-alliance flag makes defeat impossible (`:259`). This is the concrete defect the #45 naval-as-difficulty-modifier redesign is meant to fix (naval persists every phase, FIRST, and shifts the land general's Mil ¬±1 instead of being a dead-end tally). As a standalone shipped bug it sits inside the broader count-based war-end model (#45) whose replacement is post-launch ‚Äî so the immediate defect is "naval tally is dead code in the win check," fixable independently or as part of the #45 rewrite. Pairs with #45 (the redesign) + #56/#106 (generic war engine) | Either consult naval tallies in the war-end / difficulty math, or (preferred, per #45) replace the count-based win check with the War-Score model where naval persists and feeds land-battle difficulty (¬±1 Mil to the land general) rather than terminating after one win. Pairs with #45/#56/#106 | codebase + ingested (**rethinkwar (0fd0f2e5) POST 1, 16; codebase-verified `revolutionaryWar.ts:202` records navalWins/navalLosses, `:254-264` win-check uses only currentGroundWins/currentGroundLosses (+ `!frenchAlliance`), naval never read**; **‚òÖ (b38) `fixes` (a852d451 POST 10) corroborates that naval/multi-front results must be first-class: it asks for per-theater General/Admiral assignment when multiple wars run (folded on #56/#45), which presumes naval and per-theater outcomes actually feed the war state rather than being dead tallies. fixes POST 10**) |

| DH-81 | **‚òÖ (verify-and-confirm) War-end / surrender roll fires PER-BATTLE instead of once-per-TERM ‚Üí an instant game-over after a single lost battle ‚Äî GM + DESIGNER CONFIRMED (`06fbb2e5` Lars-Ark 1772)** | The 2.7 war-resolution "do we lose the war?" check is evaluated **after each individual `Battle`** rather than **once at the end of the term** on the war-score+momentum chart. In the Lars-Ark 1772 RevWar the very first land loss (Trenton, momentum ‚àí4) rolled a 40%-to-lose check ‚Üí "The United States has failed in its bid for independence!" on turn ~3 of the whole game (POST 75); the runner had to VOID the war-loss roll again and again (POST 78, 94, 118) to keep the playtest alive | **‚òÖ GM + DESIGNER CONFIRMED (not just a spreadsheet observation): ebrk85 POST 83 "I don't believe you are supposed to roll to determine if you won or lost the war after each battle. Just at the end of all the battles for that phase"; MrPotatoTed (designer) POST 85 "This is correct" (POST 86 "That is a mistake").** Root cause = STALE per-battle win/loss-threshold COLUMNS in the war doc: Ted POST 88 "Wars are no longer won by winning or losing a certain number of battles but by using the war score and momentum chart after each term"; ebrk85 POST 89 "War Charts gotta remove those columns." Correct cadence = resolve the war on the war-score+momentum chart at TERM END (the `|WS|√ó2√ó10` post-war defeat roll #45 records), with only a **50/50 "another battle this half-term?" roll per battle** (POST 88-89). ‚öÝ Tech-lead must AUDIT the SHIPPED cadence: confirm `revolutionaryWar.ts` evaluates the war-end/surrender roll once per TERM (not per `Battle` in the battle loop) and that no stale per-battle loss-threshold columns remain in any data the engine reads. The rule is designer-confirmed. ‚òÖ TECH-LEAD b43 AUDIT RESULT: the SHIPPED engine ALREADY resolves war-end ONCE PER TERM ‚Äî `runRevWarBattles` runs once per 2.7 Military phase and the win/loss check is evaluated ONCE after the battle loop (`revolutionaryWar.ts:254-264`); `revolutionaryWar.ts` never sets `gameEnded`, so a single lost battle CANNOT trigger an instant game-over in code ‚Äî **the per-battle / instant-loss symptom was the MANUAL Google-Sheets sim, NOT the build.** The REAL shipped bug = the win-path is the **STALE COUNT-THRESHOLD model** (`currentGroundWins>=7` / `currentGroundLosses>=16`, `:16-18, 254-264`), exactly the columns Ted says were replaced; the FIX = swap those threshold conditions for the war-score+momentum chart (per-term cadence is already correct; the ~66% "another battle?" continuation at `:236` is a tuning delta). So DH-81 is a win-MODEL bug, not a cadence bug ‚Äî see technical-guide debt #141. Cross-ref #45 (the war-end-roll cadence / `|WS|√ó2√ó10`), #56 (per-theater term-end resolution), #155 (the 1772-floor-must-stay-playable counter-constraint this bug makes acute) | ingested (**`06fbb2e5` (Lars and Ark) POST 75, 78, 83, 85, 86, 88, 89, 94, 118; GM-runner Ark (tier-4) + DESIGNER-AUTHORITATIVE ebrk85 (war-bug) + MrPotatoTed (POST 85-89); tech-lead to audit shipped `revolutionaryWar.ts` win/loss-check cadence; maps to #45/#56/#155**) |

| DH-83 | **‚òÖ (verify-and-confirm ‚Äî possible DRIFT, NOT a confirmed bug) Senior general/admiral "died in battle" reported by a playtester, but `revolutionaryWar.ts:87` EXPLICITLY EXCLUDES the senior general+admiral from battle-death candidates ‚Äî NEW (b45, `9acfc425` May-2023)** | **The shipped code DEATH-PROTECTS senior commanders in battle:** `applyCasualties` builds its victim pool as `[...participants].filter((p) => p.id !== war.seniorGeneralId && p.id !== war.seniorAdmiralId)` (`revolutionaryWar.ts:87`, comment "Spare senior commanders if possible (more historical)"); senior commanders instead take skill/Leadership-loss penalties on a defeat (`applyBattleLoss`), not death | **A possible drift between forum experience and current code (`9acfc425` May-2023 POST 4-5, 8): MrPotatoTed reports "The one time he [Washington] was named Senior General in a playtest I've run, he died in battle."** This CONTRADICTS the shipped death-protection at `revolutionaryWar.ts:87`. Three explanations, undecided: (a) an OLDER build before the exclusion was added; (b) a DIFFERENT mortality path coinciding with the war (natural death / a non-battle `recordDeath`); (c) a forum-rules variant. Recorded as an OPEN drift/question so it's not lost ‚Äî **NOT annotated as a confirmed DH bug** (the report cannot be reconciled to current code without more info). Also context: a player meta-bias toward AHISTORICAL outcomes (humans rarely appoint Washington as Senior General, POST 5) ‚Äî flavor, not a defect. Cross-ref #45/#56/#155 (the war formula) | OPEN QUESTION (human/tech-lead): is the death-protection at `revolutionaryWar.ts:87` the intended CURRENT rule (with POST 5's "died in battle" being an older build or a non-battle death), OR is commander battle-death intended and the exclusion a regression? Decide before treating as a bug. Cross-ref #45/#56/#155 | ingested (**`9acfc425` May-2023 (topic 2361) POST 4, 5, 8; codebase-verified senior general+admiral excluded from battle-death candidates (`revolutionaryWar.ts:87`), take stat/Leadership loss instead (`applyBattleLoss`). POSSIBLE DRIFT ‚Äî verify, do not assume a bug**) |
